title: Origin Energy Electricity Ltd — Consumer harm
investigation_objective: The AER instituted Federal Court proceedings on 19 December 2025 against Origin Energy Electricity Ltd and three other Origin subsidiaries, alleging the group continued to receive Centrepay payments from customers who had closed their accounts and fully paid what they owed, without informing or refunding them within the required timeframe — around 77,000 alleged breaches affecting over 3,400 customers between December 2019 and March 2025, totalling more than $2.5 million in retained overcharges. The AER also alleges Origin knew of the processing issue as early as 2017 and paused a process that could have prevented further overcharging, and separately failed to have adequate compliance-monitoring policies and systems in place. The AER is seeking penalties, declarations, customer remediation and a compliance program. Separately, the Federal Court found Origin Energy Electricity Ltd and other Origin entities breached hardship-customer obligations under the National Energy Retail Law/Rules on more than 100,000 occasions between January 2018 and October 2021, affecting more than 90,000 customers across NSW, the ACT, Queensland and South Australia, resulting in a $17 million penalty — the largest total penalty ever imposed for breaches of that law at the time — plus $200,000 in costs and a compliance program.
scope: Subject of Federal Court proceedings instituted by the AER on 19 December 2025, alleging around 77,000 breaches of the National Energy Retail Rules/Law; Alleged to have retained more than $2.5 million in Centrepay overcharges from over 3,400 affected customers; Alleged to have known of the systems issue as early as 2017 and to have paused a process that could have prevented further overcharging; AER separately alleges a failure to have adequate policies, systems and procedures to monitor Retail Rules compliance; Separately found to have breached customer hardship obligations on more than 100,000 occasions, affecting over 90,000 customers, resulting in a $17 million penalty
